title: Cybercrime Laws in Bangladesh: Your Rights Under the Cyber Security Act 2023
metaTitle: Cybercrime Laws Bangladesh 2026 – Cyber Security Act Rights Guide
metaDescription: Complete guide to cybercrime laws in Bangladesh — Cyber Security Act 2023, offences, penalties, how to file a case, and how to defend yourself, by a criminal lawyer in Dhaka.
heroIntro: Bangladesh's digital landscape has grown rapidly — and so have cyber-related legal disputes. Whether you are a victim of online fraud or hacking, or have been accused under the Cyber Security Act 2023, understanding your rights and the law is critical.
toc: From DSA to Cyber Security Act 2023: What Changed?
Key Offences Under the Cyber Security Act 2023
How to File a Cybercrime Complaint in Bangladesh
Your Rights If Accused Under the Cyber Security Act
Bail and Remand in Cyber Cases

sections.h2: From DSA to Cyber Security Act 2023: What Changed?
sections.content:
The Digital Security Act 2018 (DSA) was controversially repealed and replaced by the Cyber Security Act 2023 (CSA 2023), which came into force in October 2023. The government stated the replacement was intended to address human rights concerns associated with the DSA while maintaining cybercrime enforcement.

Key changes in the Cyber Security Act 2023 include:

  • Some offences that were non-bailable under the DSA were made bailable under the CSA 2023
  • Reduced maximum penalties for some categories of speech-related offences
  • Retention of core cybercrime provisions — hacking, identity theft, financial fraud, etc.
  • Provisions around online content and defamation remain, though with some modifications

The CSA 2023 is still evolving through interpretation. If you have a matter under this Act, consult a criminal lawyer in Dhaka familiar with the latest developments.

sections.h2: Key Offences Under the Cyber Security Act 2023
sections.content:
The Cyber Security Act 2023 criminalises a wide range of digital activities. Major categories include:

  • Unauthorized computer access: Accessing any computer, network, or system without permission.
  • Identity theft and impersonation: Using another person's identity online without consent.
  • Publishing obscene or defamatory content: Including content targeting individuals on social media.
  • Online fraud and financial crime: Using digital means to obtain money by deception.
  • Hacking and data theft: Unauthorized interception of communications or theft of data.
  • Spreading misinformation: Publishing false information that damages reputation or national interests.
  • Cyberbullying and harassment: Targeted online harassment of individuals.

One of the most frequently litigated areas under Bangladesh cyber law is online harassment and defamation — particularly on social media platforms like Facebook. Under the CSA 2023:

  • Publishing defamatory content about an individual online is an offence
  • Sharing intimate images without consent ("revenge porn") carries significant penalties
  • Persistent online harassment targeting an individual is criminalised

Victims should document all evidence — screenshots, URLs, timestamps — before content is removed. A criminal lawyer can help file a formal complaint with the police cyber unit or directly in the Magistrate Court.

Hacking — gaining unauthorized access to a computer system, network, or data — carries serious penalties under the CSA 2023. This includes:

  • Breaking into email accounts, social media profiles, or online banking
  • Installing malware or ransomware on another person's device
  • Intercepting electronic communications without consent
  • Acquiring passwords or access credentials by deception

Penalties can include imprisonment of up to 14 years for serious hacking offences, particularly those targeting critical infrastructure or financial systems. If you discover your systems have been compromised, legal action should be combined with cybersecurity response measures.

Online financial fraud is a rapidly growing problem in Bangladesh, including:

  • Phishing — creating fake websites or emails mimicking banks to steal credentials
  • Mobile banking fraud (bKash, Nagad scams)
  • Advance fee fraud and romance scams
  • Fraudulent e-commerce transactions

Victims should immediately contact their bank, file a police report at the local station (which may forward to the specialized Cyber Crime Unit), and engage a lawyer. Speed is critical — many fraudulent transactions can be reversed if action is taken quickly. Contact a cyber law expert in Dhaka immediately.

sections.h2: How to File a Cybercrime Complaint in Bangladesh
sections.content:
To file a cybercrime complaint in Bangladesh:

  1. Collect all evidence — screenshots, URLs, transaction records, email headers, chat logs. Preserve originals.
  2. File a General Diary (GD) at your local police station to create an official record.
  3. File a formal complaint at the specialized Bangladesh Police Cyber Crime Unit in Dhaka, or online through the national cyber complaint portal.
  4. Alternatively, file directly with the Magistrate Court if police are unresponsive.
  5. Engage a criminal lawyer to draft the complaint and guide the process.

For serious matters (large financial fraud, hacking of business systems), the Cyber Tribunal established under Bangladesh law has jurisdiction over CSA 2023 offences.

sections.h2: Your Rights If Accused Under the Cyber Security Act
sections.content:
If you are accused of a cyber offence under the CSA 2023, critical rights include:

  • Right to legal representation at all stages — exercise this immediately upon arrest or questioning.
  • Many offences under CSA 2023 are now bailable — unlike the DSA. Seek bail immediately through your lawyer.
  • Right to inspect the digital evidence against you through proper legal channels.
  • Right to challenge the investigation procedure — digital evidence must be collected, preserved, and presented according to legal standards. Improper collection can be challenged.
  • High Court petition available to challenge arrest, remand, or case on constitutional grounds.

sections.h2: Bail and Remand in Cyber Cases
sections.content:
Under the DSA 2018, many cyber offences were non-bailable — leading to lengthy pre-trial detention. The CSA 2023 made several categories bailable, which is a significant protection for the accused.

However, serious offences (e.g., hacking critical infrastructure, large-scale fraud) may still be non-bailable. If you or a family member faces arrest under the CSA 2023, contact a bail lawyer in Dhaka immediately to assess bail eligibility and file an urgent application if needed. For cases referred to the High Court, a Supreme Court advocate can seek bail from the High Court Division.

faqs.question: Is the Digital Security Act still in force in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: The DSA 2018 was repealed and replaced by the Cyber Security Act 2023 (CSA 2023), which came into force in October 2023. Old cases under the DSA may continue to be processed under the DSA.

faqs.question: Can I be arrested for a Facebook post in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Under the CSA 2023, publishing defamatory, obscene, or false information online is an offence. However, some such offences are now bailable. Consult a lawyer before making any online statements about sensitive matters.

faqs.question: What should I do if my bKash account was hacked?
faqs.answer: Immediately contact bKash and your bank to freeze transactions. File a general diary at your local police station and a complaint with the Cyber Crime Unit. Engage a lawyer to pursue recovery and criminal action.

faqs.question: Can I file a cybercrime case from outside Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: If the offence affected you in Bangladesh, you can file through a representative or once you return. Online complaint mechanisms also exist. Consult a lawyer for the best approach.

faqs.question: How long does a cyber case take in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Cases in the specialised Cyber Tribunal are intended to be resolved faster than regular courts, but in practice they can take 1-3 years for complex matters.

faqs.question: Can I record phone calls as evidence in a cyber case?
faqs.answer: Recording calls for personal use and as evidence preservation may be permitted, but using them requires careful legal handling. Your lawyer can advise on admissibility of digital evidence in Bangladeshi courts.
